CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Sep-25-2025 Continued To Apr-22-26 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration
Off Calendar Sep-05-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT BENCHLING, INC.'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION

No appearances. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: GRANTED.

Defendant has established the existence of a valid agreement to arbitrate.

Plaintiff's unconscionability argument is not supported by the record. A party claiming unconscionability must establish procedural and substantive unconscionability, but the two components are examined on a sliding scale. (Baltazar v. Forever 21, Inc. (2016) 62 Cal.4th 1237, 1243-1244.) The party claiming unconscionability has the burden of proof in establishing that the arbitration agreement is unconscionable. (OTO, L.L.C. v. Kho (2019) 8 Cal.5th 111, 125-126.)

Procedural unconscionability addresses contract negotiation and formation, focusing on "oppression or surprise due to unequal bargaining power." (Pinnacle Museum Tower Ass'n v. Pinnacle Market Development (US), LLC (2012) 55 Cal.4th 223. 246.) A court may find oppression where unequal bargaining power results in "no real negotiation and an absence of meaningful choice." (Grand Prospect Partners. v. Ross Dress for Less, Inc. (2015) 232 Cal.App.4th 1332, 1347-1348). Surprise may be found where "the challenged term is hidden in a prolix printed form or is otherwise beyond the reasonable expectation of the weaker party." (Morris v. Redwood Empire Bancorp (2005) 128 Cal.App.4th 1305, 1317.) Procedural unconscionability usually takes the form of a contract of adhesion. (Little v. Auto Stiegler, Inc. (2003) 29 Cal.4th 1064, 1071.)

Substantive unconscionability examines the fairness of the agreement's terms. (Pinnacle, supra, 55 Cal.4th at p. 246.) "California law seeks to ensure that contracts, particularly contracts of adhesion, do not impose terms that are overly harsh, unduly oppressive, or unfairly one-sided." (Lim v. Tforce Logistics, LLC (9th Cir. 2021) 8 F.4th 992, 1002.)

Here, Plaintiff has failed to establish both procedural and substantive unconscionability.

Plaintiff has demonstrated the arbitration agreement suffers from modest procedural unconscionability. The record demonstrates the arbitration agreement was an adhesion contract offered on a take-it-or-leave-it basis. Plaintiff was given no reasonable notice of the agreement or opportunity to negotiate or reject the terms of the agreement. Yet, the provision of a hyperlink to the applicable arbitration rules is permissible. Plaintiff was given time to review the document on his own. He was not forced to sign it within minutes while his employer hovered over him. Instead, he could review the documents over a four-day period. Overall, the procedural unconscionability is modest.

Plaintiff, however, fails to demonstrate meaningful substantive unconscionability. Plaintiff fails to demonstrate that anything about the agreement's terms "shock the conscience." (Sanchez v. Valencia Holding Co., LLC (2015) 61 Cal.4th 899, 910.) Plaintiff's only substantive argument that the agreement is unconscionable is that all arbitration agreements are presumed substantively unconscionable. Plaintiff cites to a California Superior Court case to support this argument and fails to include a citation to the report volume and page number for the case. The citation is improper and unpersuasive. Regardless, the argument lacks merit. Although an arbitration agreement may marginally benefit the employer in some circumstances, arbitration agreement are not as a matter of course necessarily unconscionably one-sided. Plaintiff has failed to show that this particular arbitration agreement is unconscionably one-sided.

Plaintiff's claims are ordered to arbitration, and this action is stayed pending the outcome of the arbitration. Order is signed. Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Aug-11-2025 Continued To Oct-22-25 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration
Off Calendar Jul-31-2025 Continued To Sep-05-25 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 302
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances via Zoom Conference:
Chris Quattrociocche, Esq., of The Hackett Law Firm., Tel:(213) 782-8876 for plaintiff.
BRIAN FONG, Esq., of SHEPPARD, MULLIN, RICHTER & HAMPTON LLP. Tel: (415).434.9100 for defendant.

Hearing was held. Defendant counsel contests the tentative ruling as to the dates of the proposed briefing schedule and whether Plaintiff has adequately demonstrated good cause for leave to file an opposition late. The matter discussed. The Court having reviewed the pleadings, the Court adopts its tentative ruling as modified

DEFENDANT BENCHLING, INC.'S Motion To Compel Arbitration.

The court has reviewed the moving papers, statement of non-opposition, and "response to Defendant's notice of non-opposition." The response seem to indicate an interest in filing an opposition but does not include an opposition. The hearing is continued to September 5, 2025. The opposition shall be due by August 14, 2025. The reply shall be due by August 21, 2025. A courtesy copy must be delivered to Department 302 within one (1) business day of filing. No further extensions are contemplated.

Order is Signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: M. Macadangdang; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
